elements.sent-2411-001: The personalization techniques of diffusion models succeed in generating specific concepts but also pose threats to copyright protection and illegal use.
elements.sent-2411-002: Model Watermarking is an effective method to prevent the unauthorized use of subject-driven or style-driven image generation, safeguarding concept copyrights.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, under the goal of concept-oriented protection, current watermarking schemes typically add watermarks to all images rather than applying them in a refined manner targeted at specific concepts.
elements.sent-2411-004: Additionally, the personalization techniques of diffusion models can easily remove watermarks.
elements.sent-2411-005: Existing watermarking methods struggle to achieve fine-grained watermark embedding with a few images of specific concept and prevent removal of watermarks through personalized fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-006: Therefore, we introduce a novel concept-oriented watermarking framework that seamlessly embeds imperceptible watermarks into the concept of diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-007: We conduct extensive experiments and ablation studies to verify our framework.
elements.sent-2411-009: Recent advancements in diffusion models have revolutionized the field of image generation, making it possible to create realistic images from simple text description inputs.
elements.sent-2411-010: With the growing demand for generating highly personalized and customized content, the personalization techniques \cite{shi2024instantbooth,gal2023encoder,gal2022image,kumari2023multi of diffusion models have achieved significant progress in generating novel concept descriptions (e.g., unique visual themes, motifs, stylistic preferences, or characters) with a few images of specific themes or styles.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Through powerful personalization tools like DreamBooth \cite{ruiz2023dreambooth and Lora \cite{hu2021lora , anyone, even without design skills, can create images of specific concepts using just a few personal images and simple text inputs.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\columnwidth]{1.pdf} \caption{(a) Post-processing Watermark. (b) U-net Finetuning Watermark. (c) VAE-Finetuning Watermark. (d) Latent-based Watermark. (e) Ours. } \label{fig1}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, the use of personalization techniques introduces significant risks, particularly the potential for concept misuse or leakage.
elements.sent-2411-013: Untrustworthy users can exploit these tools to create unauthorized concepts related to celebrities or artworks, leading to deepfake incidents \cite{juefei2022countering , the spread of fake news and online spam \cite{barrett2023identifying , and serious copyright violations, as the original creators' intellectual property and creativity are undermined.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: To address the ownership and accountability issues of specific concepts in generative models, recent research has introduced the task of concept watermarking \cite{feng2023catch , which aims to embed watermark information to the concept and extract the watermark from the subsequent generated images based on the watermarked concept.}
elements.sent-2411-015: In practice, the model owner typically possesses a pre-trained diffusion model and seeks to enable the generation of images for a new concept.
elements.sent-2411-016: The concept owner provides images with a specific concept to fine-tune the existing diffusion model while embedding watermark information related to the concept, enabling the model to generate images containing the new concept and allowing the watermark decoder to extract watermark information from the generated images.
elements.sent-2411-017: Even though adding watermarks to generated images has brought increasing attention \cite{kim2024wouaf,xiong2023flexible,zhang2024editguard,yang2024gaussian,fernandez2023stable,zhao2023recipe,wen2024tree,feng2024aqualora,cox2007digital , it is non-trivial for existing watermarking solutions to achieving concept watermarking.}
elements.sent-2411-018: On one hand, most existing methods \cite{wen2024tree,fernandez2023stable,zhao2023recipe typically watermark the whole images rather than images of specific concepts, so that they are inapplicable in the concept watermarking scenario.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Fine-grained watermarking mechanisms for specific concepts has rarely addressed by prior work.
elements.sent-2411-020: The major challenge is that imgae watermarking concept watermarks must not only exist within a specific image, but also remain present across various image variations generated by diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-021: Dissimilar to traditional image watermarks embedded at the pixel level, the concept watermarks appears a higher-level technical barrier as it associates the watermarks with high-levels of sematic features related to specific concepts.
elements.sent-2411-022: Since the amount of images with a specific concept generally is limited for personalization model fine-tuning by injecting a new concept, it is a great challenge for existing methods which embed watermarks with a large volume of training samples.
elements.sent-2411-023: On the other hand, robustness is another challenge for existing watermarking methods, when considering diverse adversaries in the concept protection scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-024: Recent work also showed that existing methods failed in defending model purification attacks~\cite{zhao2024can,liu2024countering , as adversaries can bypass watermarking schemes by generating various watermark-free images through personalized fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Balancing adversariality, image quality, and watermark fidelity becomes a technical obstacle.
elements.sent-2411-026: One of alternatives for increasing watermark adversariality is to inject imperceptible protective adversarial perturbations into the protected images, but this idea generally has a negative impact on both image quality and watermark fidelity~\cite{liang2023adversarial,shan2023glaze,van2023anti .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Therefore, finding out a watermarking solution to ensuring concept tracing while addressing threats from adversaries has an urgent demand.
elements.sent-2411-028: There are three core properties needed for satisfying the requirement of concept watermarking, namely, refined nature, robustness, and adversarial properties.
elements.sent-2411-029: Refined nature: The knowledge of a specific concept is learned by the denoising network (U-net), which is the core component of the diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-030: Adding a watermark to a specific concept should be done with the least amount of training data and training steps possible, while ensuring that the fidelity before and after adding the watermark remains unaffected.
elements.sent-2411-031: Robustness: The watermark should remain stable across various image processing operations and under different generation parameters.
elements.sent-2411-032: Adversarial properties: The generated concept-specific images should not be subject to ``jailbreaking" via personalized fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-033: To address the challenges outlined above, we propose a novel concept watermarking framework ConceptWm.
elements.sent-2411-034: We consider that a refined watermark training and Fidelity Preserving Perturbation are two critical factors in the context of concept watermarking.
elements.sent-2411-035: To achieve the concept watermark embedding, we propose Concept-oriented Watermarking Finetuning, which ensures the rapid learning of watermark embedding patterns in diffusion models with limited data and training budget.
elements.sent-2411-036: To ensure the robustness of the watermark against various image processing attacks and facilitate the learning of the watermark diffusion model, our Watermark Components Pretraining Module incorporates the watermark into the latent space and employs a distortion layer to ensure the watermark can withstand various image processing attacks.
elements.sent-2411-037: To effectively balance adversarial perturbation, watermark fidelity, and image quality, while mitigating the risk of adversarial attacks bypassing the watermark through personalized fine-tuning, we introduce the Fidelity Preserving Perturbation Modulation.
elements.sent-2411-038: This approach employs an alternating optimization strategy, iteratively refining both the model parameters and adversarial perturbations, thus attaining an optimal equilibrium between adversarial robustness, image quality, and watermark fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our contributions are as follows: (1) We have proposed a novel concept-driven watermarking framework that seamlessly embeds imperceptible watermarks into the concept of diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-040: We consider that a refined watermark training and fidelity preserving perturbation are two critical factors in the context of concept watermarking, which offers a fresh perspective on how diffusion model watermarking can be applied in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-041: Through the alternating optimization of model training and adversarial algorithms, it effectively balances adversarial robustness, image quality, and watermark fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-043: Given the substantial computational requirements of training Stable Diffusion from scratch, many methods aim to inject specific concepts into the model through fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-044: Currently used fine-tuning techniques include Textual Inversion \cite{gal2022image , DreamBooth \cite{ruiz2023dreambooth and LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)\cite{hu2021lora .}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Textual Inversion focuses solely on training text embeddings during fine-tuning, allowing concepts to be injected into the text encoder without altering the weights of the U-Net.
elements.sent-2411-046: DreamBooth, on the other hand, fine-tunes the entire U-Net portion of the Stable Diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-047: Unlike conventional text-to-image fine-tuning, DreamBooth employs prior loss during training to prevent overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-048: LoRA achieves quick and lightweight fine-tuning of Stable Diffusion by training incremental weights in the attention layers of the U-Net.
elements.sent-2411-049: Model Watermarking for Latent Diffusion Models.
elements.sent-2411-050: WDM \cite{zhao2023recipe trains an autoencoder to stamp a watermark on all training data before re-training the generator from scratch.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Aqualora \cite{feng2024aqualora propose a watermark Lora method to watermark all the images.}
elements.sent-2411-052: VAE-based methods \cite{xiong2023flexible,kim2024wouaf,fernandez2023stable fine-tune VAE-Decoder to ensure that all generated images contain the watermark.}
elements.sent-2411-053: However, the aforementioned watermarking schemes apply watermarks to all generated images, leaving a gap in the protection of specific styles.
elements.sent-2411-054: Meanwhile, A large number of images is often required for the model to learn the watermark embedding pattern, but concept-oriented images for finetuning are often limited.
elements.sent-2411-056: To safeguard personal images, such as faces and artwork, from potential infringement during the fine-tuning of Stable Diffusion, recent studies focus on disrupting the fine-tuning process by adding imperceptible protective noise to these images.
elements.sent-2411-057: AdvDM \cite{liang2023adversarial introduces a direct adversarial attack on Stable Diffusion by maximizing the Mean Squared Error loss during optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-058: This method employs adversarial noise to safeguard personal images.
elements.sent-2411-059: Anti-DreamBooth \cite{van2023anti integrates the DreamBooth fine-tuning process of Stable Diffusion into its framework, designing a bi-level min-max optimization approach to generate protective perturbations.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Additionally, other studies \cite{wu2023towards,ye2023duaw,zhao2023unlearnable,zheng2023understanding have investigated the use of similar adversarial perturbation techniques to generate protective noise for images.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Conditional denoising model, denoted as $\epsilon_\theta(x,t,c)$, can generate images based on a given text condition $c$.
elements.sent-2411-063: During training, a loss function $\mathcal{L _{cond}$ is used to guide the diffusion model in denoising the latent representations, $ x_{t =\sqrt{\bar{\alpha _{t+1 x_{0 +\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha _{t+1 \epsilon_{\theta (x_{t ) $.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Given the input image $x_0$, each variable $x_t$ is constructed via injecting noise at corresponding timestep $t$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The objective can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}0.9\hsize{!}{ $ \mathcal{L}_{cond}(\theta,x_0)=\mathbb{E}_{x_0,t,c,\epsilon\in\mathcal{N}(0,1)}\|\epsilon-\epsilon_\theta(x_{t+1},t,c)\|_2^2.$ } \label{diff1}\end{equation} DreamBooth.
elements.sent-2411-066: DreamBooth employs the base loss of diffusion models in Eq.\ref{diff1 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: It also incorporates a prior preservation loss to avoid overfitting and text-shifting issues, particularly when only a limited set of instance examples is available.
elements.sent-2411-068: More precisely, it uses a generic prior prompt such as ``a photo of [class]" and learn the concept with the prompt such as ``a photo of \textless concept \textgreater [class]".
elements.sent-2411-069: The objective can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}1\hsize{!}{ $\mathcal{L}_{ft}(\theta,x_0)=\mathbb{E}_{x_0,t}\|\epsilon-\epsilon_\theta(x_{t+1},t,c)\|_2^2 +\lambda\|\epsilon'-\epsilon_\theta(x_{t+1}',t,c_{cls})\|_2^2,$ } \label{eqa2}\end{equation} where $\epsilon$ and $\epsilon'$ are both sampled from the standard normal distribution $\mathcal{N (0,\mathbf{I )$, the noisy variable $x_{t + 1 '$ is generated from the original stable diffusion model $\theta_{\text{ori }$ using the prior prompt $c_{\text{cls }$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: The term $\lambda$ emphasizes the importance of the prior term in the generation process.. Adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-071: The goal of adversarial attacks is to find imperceptible perturbations to the input image that mislead the behavior of a given model.
elements.sent-2411-072: Typical research usually focuses on classification tasks, where, for a model $f$, an adversarial example $x'$ of an input image $x$ is generated such that it is visually indistinguishable, while causing a misclassification $y = f(x')$ .
elements.sent-2411-073: The perturbation is typically constrained to lie within an $\eta$-ball to ensure minimal visual difference, specifically satisfying $\|x' - x\|_p < \eta$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) \cite{madry2017towards is a widely used attack method that relies on an iterative optimization process.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The procedure to update and predict $\tilde{x}$ for an untargeted attack is as follows: \begin{equation}\tilde{x}^{t+1}=\Pi_{(x,\eta)}\left(\tilde{x}^{t}+\alpha\cdot sign(\nabla_{x}\mathcal{L}(f(x),y))\right),\end{equation} where $\Pi_{x, \eta (z)$ restricts the pixel values of $ z $ within an $ \eta $-ball around the original values in $ x $.}

elements.sent-2411-076: Challenges of existing methods: (1) How to achieve concept-oriented watermark fine-tuning of U-net with limited data and training budget? We will solve this via the framework design in Sec.~\ref{fine-tune .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Integrating the existing protective perturbation method into the watermarking mechanism and embedding it into the model while ensuring good generation quality is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-078: This will be solved in Sec.. \ref{Modulation .}
elements.sent-2411-079: The main objective of our method is to protect images of the specific concept generated by the diffusion model by embedding adversarial noise and the watermark pattern into the U-net component of the diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-080: Figure \ref{fig2 illustrates an overview of our approach.}
elements.sent-2411-081: The Watermark Components Pretraining module implements watermarking in the latent space and enhances the robustness of the watermark through a distortion layer.
elements.sent-2411-082: Fidelity Preserving Perturbation Modulation implements adversarial perturbations to the image while balancing watermarking, image quality, and adversarial properties.
elements.sent-2411-083: Concept-oriented Watermark Fine-tuning enables learning for specific concepts and integrates adversarial watermarks through fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-084: Existing image watermarking methods often face significant differences between the watermark embedding pattern and the latent space, which causes the watermark information to be disrupted or even lost when transformed into the latent space by the VAE encoder or diffusion regeneration.
elements.sent-2411-085: This issue has been discussed in related watermarking attack methods \cite{an2024benchmarking,balle2018variational,cheng2020learned .}
elements.sent-2411-086: Our objective is to embed a robust watermark within the image that is both resilient and conducive to efficient learning by the U-Net architecture.
elements.sent-2411-087: The U-Net learns the distribution and noise of the image in the latent space, and embedding the watermark into the latent space enables the model to learn the consistency between the watermark and the generated images.
elements.sent-2411-088: In this step, the watermarked images are processed through the message decoder to evaluate the discrepancy between the decoded watermark information and the original content of the watermarked image.
elements.sent-2411-089: The message loss is the Binary Cross Entropy (BCE) between the message $m$ and the sigmoid $\sigma(m')$. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_m =-\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}m_k\log\sigma(m_k')+(1-m_k)\log(1-\sigma(m_k')) \end{aligned}\end{equation} $I_0$ is the original image.
elements.sent-2411-090: To ensure visual consistency, we use the LPIPS loss function \cite{zhang2018unreasonable to evaluate the global loss between the watermarked image and the reconstructed image.}
elements.sent-2411-091: To avoid excessive modification of local image regions, we select the most prominent parts of the image and calculate channel loss. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} R es(\mathbf{I}_{0},\mathbf{I}_{r}) = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{c=1}^{3} |{\mathbf{I}_c}^0(i,j)-{\mathbf{I}_c}^r(i,j)| \mathcal{L}_{channel}=\sum_{k=1}^K\left[\operatorname{Top-k}\left(\operatorname{Res}(\mathbf{I}_0,\mathbf{I}_r)\right)\right] \end{aligned}\end{equation} Our training objective can be summarized as follows, where $\lambda$ and $\mu$ represent the coefficients: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{total}=\mathcal{L}_m+\lambda\mathcal{L}_{pips}+\mu\mathcal{L}_{channel}\end{equation} The trained message-encoder and message-decoder will collaborate with the Fidelity Preserving Perturbation Modulation module to assist the U-Net in learning the adversarial watermark.
elements.sent-2411-092: At this stage, the watermark is embedded into specific concepts and then injected into the U-Net.
elements.sent-2411-093: The main challenges are as follows: The fine-tuned images for specific concepts are often limited, making it difficult for the model to learn the specific watermark patterns; Due to the introduction of adversarial perturbations, directly using the DreamBooth training paradigm will be affected by protective perturbations, preventing the model from learning the concept.
elements.sent-2411-094: The limited number of specific concept samples and adversarial perturbation often result in failures when directly adding an adversarial watermark into images and deploying DreamBooth.
elements.sent-2411-095: This can prevent the watermark embedding pattern from being learned effectively and lead to quality degradation.
elements.sent-2411-096: Our core idea is to decouple concept learning from adversarial watermark learning.
elements.sent-2411-097: In the DreamBooth stage, we learn the specific concept, and by adding adversarial noise to the same image and also using the image without the adversarial noise, the model learns to differentiate between them.
elements.sent-2411-098: The training objective of this stage is as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{ft}}(\theta) :=\mathbb{E}_{t,c,x}\Big[\|\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta(x_t+\varphi,t,c)-\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{ori}(x_t,t,c\big)\|^2\Big]\end{aligned}\end{equation} $\varphi$ represents the adversarial perturbation containing the watermark generated through Fidelity Preserving Perturbation Modulation, $\epsilon_{ori}$ denotes the fixed model, and $\epsilon_\theta$ refers to the trained model.
elements.sent-2411-099: The goal of this fine-tuning is to maximize the loss of $L_{cond}$ in Eq. \ref{diff1 and minimize the loss of $L_{ft}$ \ref{eqa2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The adversarial requirement for watermarking is to disrupt the learning process of DreamBooth.
elements.sent-2411-101: Since the DreamBooth model tends to overfit on adversarial images, this can be exploited to make it perform worse when reconstructing clean images. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \sigma^{*(i)} =\underset{\delta^{(i)}}{\arg \max } \mathcal{L}_{\text {cond }}(\theta^{*}, x^{(i)}), \forall i \in\{1, . ., N_{ft}\}, \text { s.t. } \theta^{*}=\underset{\theta}{\arg \min } \sum_{i=1}^{N_{ft}} \mathcal{L}_{ft}(\theta, x^{(i)}+\sigma^{(i)}), \text { and } \|\sigma^{(i)}\|_{j} \leq \eta \quad \forall i \in\{1, . ., N_{ft}\} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $x^i$ denotes the clean samples.

elements.sent-2411-102: Meanwhile, we need to ensure that the image quality and watermark loss are as minimal as possible.

elements.sent-2411-108: Each image is resized and center-cropped to a resolution of 512 × 512.. Training Configurations.

elements.sent-2411-111: The training instance prompt for style-driven generation is "a painting in the style of \textless concept \textgreater" and the prior prompt is "a painting".
elements.sent-2411-112: For PGD, we use $\alpha$ = 1e-5 and the default noise budget $\eta$ = 1e-3.
elements.sent-2411-113: The learning rate of the dreambooth is set 5e-6.. Evaluation Metrics.

elements.sent-2411-216: We introduce Fidelity-preserving Latent Watermarking (FLW) to generate latent watermarks based on image characteristics and the Adversarial Watermarking Modulation module to prevent "jailbreaking" via personalized finetuning.

elements.sent-2411-224: Concept watermarking technique is considered to be an alternative to address ownership and accountability issues of specific concepts in generative model, which aims to embed watermark information into the concept and extract the watermarks from subsequent generated images on the basis of the watermarked concept \cite{feng2023catch .}
elements.sent-2411-225: In general, the model owner possesses a pre-trained diffusion model and aims to generate images for a new concept.
elements.sent-2411-226: The concept owner provides images of the specific concept to fine-tune the existing diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-227: During this fine-tuning process, watermark information related to the concept is embedded, enabling the model to generate images with the new concept.
elements.sent-2411-228: A watermark decoder can extract the watermark information from the generated images.
elements.sent-2411-229: However, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig1 , existing watermarking methods have not yet to achieve concept watermarking, despite the growing interest in adding watermarks to generated images \cite{kim2024wouaf,xiong2023flexible,zhang2024editguard,yang2024gaussian,fernandez2023stable,zhao2023recipe,wen2024tree,feng2024aqualora,cox2007digital .}}
elements.sent-2411-230: On the one hand, prior work has rarely addressed fine-grained watermarking mechanisms for specific concepts.
elements.sent-2411-231: Most existing methods \cite{wen2024tree,fernandez2023stable,zhao2023recipe watermark the entire images rather than images of specific concepts, making them inapplicable for concept watermarking scenario.}
elements.sent-2411-232: A major challenge in concept watermarking is to ensure that the watermark not only exists within a specific image but also remains present across various image variations genraeted by the diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-233: Compared to traditional image watermarks embedded at the pixel level, concept watermarks appears a higher-level technical barrier, as it associates the watermarks with high-level sematic features related to specific concepts.
elements.sent-2411-234: Since the number of images with a specific concept is limited, embedding watermarks in personalization models is a great challenge, especially when fine-tuning the model by injecting a new concept.
elements.sent-2411-235: On the other hand, robustness remains a challenge for current watermarking methods in concept protection scenarios, when considering diverse adversaries.
elements.sent-2411-236: Recent studies have demonstrated that existing methods are ineffective against model purification attacks~\cite{zhao2024can,liu2024countering , as adversaries can bypass watermarking schemes by generating watermark-free images through personalized fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-237: The technical challenge lies in balancing adversariality, image quality, and watermark fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-238: One potential approach to enhancing watermark adversariality is to inject imperceptible protective adversarial perturbations into images, even though this type of methods frequently compromises both image quality and watermark fidelity~\cite{liang2023adversarial,shan2023glaze,van2023anti .}
elements.sent-2411-239: Therefore, there is an urgent need for watermarking solution that ensures concept tracing while effectively addressing adversarial threats.
elements.sent-2411-240: Our investigation finds three fundamental properties required by model watermarking, including refined nature, robustness, and adversarial properties.
elements.sent-2411-241: To be specific, refined nature, as a core component of diffusion model, requires the ability to embed a watermark into a specific concept through minimal training data and steps without compromising fidelity, as the knowledge of a specific concept generally is learnt by the denoising network (U-Net).
elements.sent-2411-242: Robustness ensures that the watermark remains stable across various image processing operations and generation parameters.
elements.sent-2411-243: Adversarial properties typically guarantee that the generated concept-specific images cannot be easily ``jailbreaken" via personalized fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-244: To address these challenges, we propose a novel concept watermarking framework, named ConceptWM, which focuses on two critical factors, namely refined watermark training and adversarial perturbation modulation.
elements.sent-2411-245: To achieve the concept watermark embedding, we develop a Efficient Concept Watermark Finetuning (ECWF) scheme, which rapidly learns watermark embedding patterns in diffusion models with limited data and training resources.
elements.sent-2411-246: Additionally, we develop a Fidelity-preserving Latent Watermark (FLW) module that incorporates the watermark into the latent space and employs a distortion layer in order to enhance robustness against image processing attacks.
elements.sent-2411-247: Next, we propose an Adversarial Watermark Modulation (AWM) to balance adversarial perturbation, watermark fidelity, and image quality, while mitigating the risk of adversarial attacks bypassing the watermark through personalized fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-248: This scheme utilizes an alternating optimization strategy to refine both referenced model parameters and adversarial perturbations, so that an optimal equilibrium among adversarial robustness, image quality, and watermark fidelity can be achieved.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Efficient Concept Watermark Finetuning} \label{algorithm: cwf} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \algrenewcommand\algorithmicfunction{experiment} \algrenewtext{EndFunction}{} \Function {$CWF$}{$X_A$,$X_B$,$\theta$,E} \For{ $x_0 \in X_A$} \State c=`` a photo of $\langle$ concept $\rangle$ person " \State $\theta = \operatorname{TrainOneStepDreamBooth}(\theta,x_0,c)$ \State $\theta^{*} \leftarrow \text { copy parameters from } \theta$ \State $\varphi=AWM(x_0,X_B,\theta)$ \State $t\sim\mathrm{Uniform}(\{1,\ldots,T\})$ \State $z_0=E(x_0)$ \State $z_t\leftarrow\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}z_0+\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ \State $z_t^\mathrm{w}\leftarrow\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}(z_0+\varphi)+\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ \State Take gradient step on $\nabla_{\theta} \|{\epsilon_{\theta}}(z_t^\mathrm{w},t,c)-{\epsilon_{\theta^{*}}}(z_t,t,c)\|^2$ \EndFor \EndFunction \Return $\theta$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-366: The personalization techniques of diffusion models succeed in generating images with specific concepts.
elements.sent-2411-367: This ability also poses great threats to copyright protection and network security since malicious users can generate unauthorized content and disinformation relevant to a target concept.
elements.sent-2411-368: Model watermarking is an effective solution to trace the malicious generated images and safeguard their copyright.
elements.sent-2411-369: However, existing model watermarking techniques merely achieve image-level tracing without concept traceability.
elements.sent-2411-370: When tracing infringing or harmful concepts, current approaches execute image concept detection and model tracing sequentially, where performance is critically constrained by concept detection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-371: In this paper, we propose a lightweight concept watermarking framework that efficiently binds target concepts to model watermarks, supporting simultaneous concept identification and model tracing via single-stage watermark verification.
elements.sent-2411-372: To further enhance the robustness of concept watermarking, we propose an adversarial perturbation injection method collaboratively embedded with watermarks during image generation, avoiding watermark removal by model purification attacks.
elements.sent-2411-373: Experimental results demonstrate that ConceptWM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art watermarking methods, improving detection accuracy by 6.3\%-19.3\% across diverse datasets including COCO and StableDiffusionDB.
elements.sent-2411-374: Additionally, ConceptWM possesses a critical capability absent in other watermarking methods: it sustains a 21.7\% FID/CLIP degradation under adversarial fine-tuning of Stable Diffusion models on WikiArt and CelebA-HQ, demonstrating its capability to mitigate model misuse.
elements.sent-2411-375: Recent advance in diffusion models have significantly improved image generation ability, enabling the creation of high-quality images from personalized textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-376: The upgraded diffusion models achieve few-shot learning for new visual concept generation, such as unique visual themes, motifs, stylistic preferences, or celibrities, using only a few images with the target concepts \cite{shi2024instantbooth,gal2023encoder,gal2022image,kumari2023multi .}
elements.sent-2411-377: However, these techniques pose significant risks for visual concept infringement and misuse.
elements.sent-2411-378: For example, a malevolent actor might leverage a few online trademark or digital artwork images to fine-tune off-the-shelf diffusion models and edit the content without permission \cite{zhu2024watermark,li2025towards .}
elements.sent-2411-379: Similarly, a few online celebrity images are adequate for malevolent actors to generate misinformation with these celebrities \cite{wang2024simac .}
elements.sent-2411-380: It becomes an urgent issue to effectively trace AI-edited visual concept images for copyright protection and privacy preservation.

(b) VAE finetuning watermark. (c) U-Net finetuning watermark. (d) Latent-based watermark. (e) Concept-oriented watermark. } \label{fig1}
elements.sent-2411-381: Model watermarking is a representative way to trace the source model of generated images and thus verify the accountability of malicious content.
elements.sent-2411-382: The typical solutions of model watermarking fine-tune the pre-trained diffusion models to inject watermarks and extrect the watermark information from any generated images by a watermark decoder.
elements.sent-2411-383: Compared with post processing solutions by adding watermarks after image generation (Fig. \ref{fig1 (a)), model watermarking is more secure to avoid watermark evasion \cite{zhao2023generative .}}
elements.sent-2411-384: Despite the great progress in diverse model watermarking schemes (Fig. \ref{fig1 (b)(c)(d)), existing schemes indiscriminately inject watermarks into all the generated images agnostic to visual concepts, which has two obvious limitations for visual concept tracing and protection.}
elements.sent-2411-385: On the one hand, tracing concept-level malicious image becomes indirect that requires a two-stage pipeline: initial detection of target visual concepts in images, followed by watermark-based tracing for concept images.
elements.sent-2411-386: Thus, the tracing effectiveness is critically constrained by concept detection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-387: One the other hand, recent studies \cite{cui2025ft reveal that existing personalization methods enable malicious actors to remove watermarks by fine-tuning diffusion models with a few watermarked samples, while retaining concept generation capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-388: Therefore, current model watermarking methods with only image tracing capability are still unable to prevent misue of watermarked images.
elements.sent-2411-389: In this paper, we propose an approach that aims to trace and protect generated images with target visual concepts by diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-390: The core idea of our approach is Concept Watermarking, that is, during image generation, we embed both traceable watermarks and fine-tuning-resistent perturbations into concept-specific generated images.
elements.sent-2411-391: This paradigm enables people to technically govern AI-powered malicous content and proactively defend subsequent misuse of generated content in a finer manner.
elements.sent-2411-392: To this end, three new challenges need to be addressed: First, for a target concept (e.g., `a specific kind A of cats'), concept watermarking requires to establish tripartite correlation among a concept watermark (e.g., a bit sequence `01100'), visual concepts (diverse appreances of `A cats') in the generated images, and a textual concept (a psuedo word of `cat A') in the prompts, which is quite different from existing watermarking methods that solely establish bipartite correlation between a watermark and model outputs; Second, concept watermarking has adversarial capability that the generated concept images are unable to be used for model fine-tuning, which has no influence on concept-irrelevant generated images; Third, concept watermarking is robust to diverse real-world attacks including image processing, geometric transformations, and model regeneration attacks.
elements.sent-2411-393: To address these challenges, we propose a lightweight concept watermarking framework, named ConceptWM, which contains two main componets, i.e., efficient concept watermark training and imperceptible adversarial perturbation injection.
elements.sent-2411-394: To embed a watermark that is both fidelity-preserving and conducive to learning by U-Net, we propose a fidelity-preserving latent watermark module that incorporates the watermark into the latent space and introduce a fusion layer after the message encoder and latent concatenation to mitigate rigid watermark pattern artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-395: To achieve concept-level image tracing, we propose an efficient concept watermark training strategy that alternately optimizes DreamBooth fine-tuning and watermark embedding training to achieve joint concept-watermark learning.
elements.sent-2411-396: Our method learns watermark embeddings efficiently, requiring only a few reference images and low training budgets.
elements.sent-2411-397: To prevent the misuse of watermarked images, we propose an imperceptible adversarial perturbation injection module to refine both referenced model parameters and adversarial perturbations by proposed optimization strategy, which balances adversarial robustness, watermark fidelity, and image quality, while mitigating the attack risk by personalized fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-398: Our contributions are as follows: (1) We propose a lightweight concept watermarking framework that imperceptibly embeds watermarks into the generated concept-specific images by diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-399: Concept watermarking first tackles the challenge of establishing tripartite correlation across three modalities, e.g., watermarks, visual concept and textual concept, which makes it possible for concept-level generated image tracing in one-stop manner.
elements.sent-2411-400: This mechanism enables the adversarial perturbation injection without interference to image quality and watermark fidelity, while making the generated concept images resistent to malicious use for model fine-tuning.
